sections.title: From Demo to Budget Line
works: the accounting-not-benchmark framing is a strong, specific opening idea
the $13.8B / 6x figure lands early and is sourced
clarity: 'essentially complete' is an overclaim for 2026 — soften to 'largely complete' so a skeptic doesn't bounce
flow: good handoff into s2 with 'the money is not where the demos are' — keep that thread explicit
evidence: fine — the single sourced figure carries the section

line_edits.why: calibrated confidence reads as more credible to a skeptical investor than an absolute

sections.title: Follow the Money, Not the Models
works: two-number structure is clean and persuasive
'racing to zero' is a vivid, correct framing
clarity: 'undifferentiated' is jargon-adjacent — fine for this audience, keep
flow: the 'put those together' transition is doing real work; keep it
evidence: 280x figure should name the time window inline so it can't be read as cherry-picked → already present, good
style: 'a bad place to be the fifth entrant' is a nice sharp aside — protect it in the voice pass

line_edits.suggested: margin migrates up the stack — toward whoever turns a raw model into a dependable workflow.
line_edits.why: completes the logical chain into s3 (reliability) instead of leaving it implicit

sections.title: Reliability Is the Whole Game
works: the 90%-per-step → fails-the-whole-task point is the sharpest argument in the piece
Klarna proof-point converts the abstract claim into a P&L
clarity: spell out why sub-50% autonomous success matters operationally — the per-step math does this, keep it adjacent to the stat
evidence: could add one more proof-point, but one named, quantified example is enough for a 2,000-word piece — do not pad
style: 'That is not a demo. That is a P&L line.' — keep the staccato, it's the voice

line_edits.original: It is the system that makes an ordinary model trustworthy
line_edits.suggested: It is the system that makes an ordinary model trustworthy enough to hand a real workflow
line_edits.why: ties 'trustworthy' to the concrete thing the buyer is paying for

sections.title: Will Agents Eat the SaaS Seat?
works: pressure-testing rather than repeating the thesis is exactly right for this reader
the qualified verdict is the most credible passage in the draft
clarity: 'apocalypse' in the CTA line is fun but slightly flip — judgment call, lean toward keeping the voice
flow: the turn from 'incumbents take it seriously' to 'but the evidence qualifies it' is the rhetorical hinge of the piece — keep it sharp
evidence: the qualifier (C7) is synthesized commentary — make sure the references list flags it as analysis, not a hard stat
style: 'Underwrite the repricing, not the apocalypse' is a keeper line

sections.title: The Investable Layer
works: concrete underwrite/avoid guidance is what the investor came for
ties the moat directly to switching cost and proprietary data
flow: lands the CTA cleanly into the conclusion
evidence: the 'thin GPT wrapper' avoid is an opinion — fine, the piece earned it by here

overall: Strong, sourced, and genuinely contrarian where it counts. Two small calibration edits (s1 overclaim, s2 transition) and protect the staccato voice lines through the voice pass. No structural changes needed; do not pad the evidence.
source: content-research-writer step 5 — Section-by-Section Feedback (What Works + Clarity/Flow/Evidence/Style + specific line edits per section)
